texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Increase in the atmospheric concentration of carbon dioxide (CO2) is being accepted as one of the most imminent threats to the mankind, since it is known as the major greenhouse gas. From the mitigation standpoint of CO2 emissions, a countermeasure to reduce the CO2 emissions is carbon dioxide capture and storage (CCS). The CO2 capture technology separates CO2 from the rest of the target gas stream.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present paper, we report experimental results from an ammonia-based carbon dioxide (CO2) capture process operation. A pilot facility of 1000 Nm 3 /h to remove CO2 from blast furnace gas (BFG, ∼ 23% of CO2) has been constructed in an iron-making workplace, resulting in the CO2 production of 10 t-CO2/d. Steam generated from waste heat recovery system has been successfully supplied to the CO2 removal unit for regeneration of absorbent solution and concentration of ammonia. Technical performance shows that the facility can capture CO2 at the rate of 90% with the purity of 99% in the product CO2 stream. Results of process simulations and process operation confirmed the improvement of process economics through heat integration and pressurized absorption. Thermal energy requirement for the regeneration of absorbent solution could be lowered to 2.5 GJ/t-CO2. Accordingly, the ammonia slip has been reduced to the level of ∼ 10 ppm in both columns of absorption and regeneration. From this study we found that the technology could be a competitive option for the economic reduction of CO2 emissions in large stationary point sources such as in iron and steel industry. Conclusively, we insist that the developed technology is extremely functional if there exists waste heat at low temperature.

texts.orig: in industries, the energy requirement for regeneration must be drastically reduced to make it a viable solution to be employed for CO2 capture technology. Ammonia-based CO2 capture technology is considered as an alternative to conventional amine-based CO2 capture technology mainly due to its high CO2 loading capacity, low regeneration temperature, low regeneration energy consumption, and low corrosiveness. Some of the key characteristics that ammonia-based CO2 capture technology delivers economic advantages over amine-based CO2 capture technology include (1) the low temperature regeneration resulting in lower thermal energy requirement temperature and (2) the low energy requirement for regeneration. A recent review discusses the current issues and status of the development of ammonia-based CO2 capture process stating that the technology is at the pilot testing stage (Han et al., 2013).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The motivation of selecting aqueous ammonia as absorbent solution is to develop a CO2 capture technology utilizing resources in the working environment. The aqueous ammonia process for capturing CO2 from BFG has been studied at Research Institute of Industrial Science & Technology (RIST) since 2006 using lab scale CO2 capture facilities (2 Nm 3 /h), 1st stage pilot plant (50 Nm 3 -BFG/h) and the current pilot plant (1000 Nm 3 -BFG/h) consecutively. We have chosen the aqueous ammonia as the absorbent chemical, since it can be regenerated at very low temperature, ∼ 80 ◦ C. The thermal energy can be supplied by recovering waste heat at low temperature such as boiler stack, producing low grade steam. This work is a continuation of our previous studies on CO2 capture technology development using aqueous ammonia (Kim et al., 2009; Rhee et al., 2011). The present study includes sufficient experimental data to evaluate the process performance with high accuracy. Operation results using the pilot facility are to be reported and discussed from the standpoints of both technology itself and economics.

texts.orig: 2.1. Technical aspect
texts.text: 2.1. Technical aspect

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following the previous discussion on the solvent-based CO2 capture technology, a comparison among capture technologies using amines, ammonia and potassium carbonates was made in Table 1. Slower absorption kinetics and high volatility are the two main demerits of ammonia-based CO2 capture. However, ammonia-based CO2 capture technology is superior to aminebased CO2 capture technology in such characteristics as low regeneration temperature (accordingly, lower regeneration energy requirements), high loading/capture capacity, low corrosiveness, and high thermal stability. Key technical performance results such as the CO2 removal efficiency (defined in Section 3.3) and purity of CO2 were satisfactory for the amine-based (Iijima et al., 2012) and ammonia-based technologies (Alstom, 2011). A typical quality specification on CO2 purity for CO2-EOR is known to be 95% minimum (with water content of 480 mg/m 3 maximum and H2S of 10-200 ppm maximum) which lies in the quite achievable range for the current CO2 capture technologies (Advanced Resources International, 2011).

The core interest among the researchers is the chemistry of the absorption and desorption of CO2, which in turn leads to the understanding of thermal energy requirement of absorbent regeneration. The chemical reactions in equilibrium and their respective heat of reaction values are given in Eqs. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Economic aspect
texts.text: 2.2. Economic aspect

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Economics of the process can be viewed from the combination of the capital investment and the operational and maintenance expenditure. Excluding the maintenance and administrative expenditure, the operating expenditure is mainly composed of the cost of electricity consumption, thermal energy requirement, water consumption, chemical loss, wastes disposal, etc. The major thermal energy requirement for absorbent-based system comes from the regeneration energy of absorbent chemical. Although not being discussed in detail, the other thermal energy component, the heat requirement for sour water treatment, can be the concentration energy of ammonia or the ammonia recovery energy, i.e. the thermal energy requirement for the distillation of aqueous carbonated ammonia to produce ammonia vapor and stripped water vapor so that they can be reused within the whole process. The function of ammonia concentration or recovery can be considered as an equivalent concept of reclaiming in the amine-based processes. As a consequence, the total thermal energy requirement ( Qthermal ) for the ammonia-based CO2 capture processes is made up of regeneration and concentration energy (Eq. (6)).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The regeneration energy for the CO2 capture facility is mainly composed of three components, namely the solvent heating energy, the water vaporization (steam stripping) energy, and the desorption energy. More precisely, it is composed of sensible (heating up of rich aqueous ammonia solution, Qsens ), latent (boiling of aqueous ammonia, Qlat ), and desorption energy (regeneration of aqueous ammonia, resulted in lean aqueous ammonia solution, Qdes ) as in Eq. (7). In general, the majority of the regeneration energy comes from the desorption enthalpy for stripping CO2 from the absorbent. However, unlike the amine-based process, although it depends on the ammonia concentration, the critical portion of regeneration energy component for the ammonia-based process can be the sensible heat of the solvent. The reason for such a distinct feature comes from the fact that the temperature of solvent regeneration is lower than the boiling point of water, i.e. literally there is no need for water vaporization.

texts.orig: As stated above, aqueous ammonia-based CO2 capture processes are being developed around the globe. However, there have been few published works on the thermal energy requirement for the process at pilot facility (Yu et al., 2011), though a simulation study predicts it lower than 2.5 GJ/t-CO2 (Darde et al., 2012). It is noteworthy that the energy requirement for the pre-treatment such as cooling of the feed gas and the post-treatment such as the waste chemicals disposal is hardly dealt with. Noting that the economics should be viewed from the entire energy requirement it is suggested to investigate the economics as a whole including the two. Recent studies indicate that the regeneration energy employing the amine-based CO2 capture technology be less than 3 GJ/t-CO2; 2.5 GJ/t-CO2 for KS-1 solvent (Iijima et al., 2011) and 3.1 GJ/t-CO2 for RITE-B solvent (Goto et al., 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: BFG, one of the major by-product gases in iron industry, is being used as a feed gas for the testing. The CO2 concentration in typical BFG is 20-25 wt% which is twice as high as that of flue gas from a typical coal-fired power plant. Aqueous ammonia with 9 wt% of ammonia has been used to minimize the ammonia slip. The absorbent solution is prepared by mixing process water to the high concentration aqueous ammonia ( ∼ 25 wt%, Daejung Chemicals, Korea). The concentration of aqua ammonia is measured regularly, thus adding the high concentration ammonia if needed.

texts.orig: Two different types of packings, nominally random packing and structured packing, are selected to test the performance. The internal packings, SP-Type3 for structured packing and Casecade rings for random packing, were provided by AMT Pacific, Republic of Korea. For different employment of the packings, HETP (Height Equivalent to Theoretical Plates) were set to be the same. We investigated the two different packing to see if plastic random packing can be used, since it costs less resulting in the lower initial investment cost.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The intrinsic nature of reboiling the absorbent solutions at relatively low temperature (around 80 ◦ C) makes the ammonia-based CO2 capture technology functional by utilizing the low-grade steam which can be generated by recovering waste heat at low-( ∼ 150 ◦ C) and mid-temperature ( ∼ 300 ◦ C). An exemplary industry is the iron and steel industry, since it consumes huge amount energy and accordingly produces waste heat at various temperatures. However, large portion of waste heat at lowand mid-temperature is not recovered due to the economic reason. The overall process schematic showing the integrated system of the CO2 capture facility and the waste heat recovery system is depicted in Fig. 2. The process utilizes the recovered waste heat via steam generation. Waste heat of flue gas leaving the boiler stacks at around 150 ◦ C was recovered and steam was produced. Then the steam was supplied to the CO2 capture pilot facility as reboiling energy for the regeneration and concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the absorption process, CO2 in BFG reacts with ammonia (NH3) and water and is converted into ammonium salts/ions such as ammonium carbamate (NH2COONH4), ammonium carbonate ((NH4)2CO3) and ammonium bicarbonate (NH4HCO3) and other ionic species. After passing through the absorber, the CO2-treated BFG undergoes washing for the prevention of ammonia slip at the top of the absorber, and it finally leaves the absorber. The CO2-rich solution at the bottom of the absorber is pumped to the middle of the regenerator via a heat exchanger with the CO2-lean solution from the regenerator. In the regeneration column, the chemical reactions are reversed by the supplied heat to the reboiler maintaining the regeneration condition. At temperatures around 80 ◦ C the ammonium salts (i.e. ammonium carbonate, ammonium bicarbonate and ammonium carbamate) readily decompose to free ammonia, water, CO2 and/or other ionic species. In the regenerator, the ammonia vapor and CO2 gas are to be separated, allowing recovery of high concentrations of CO2. The regenerated CO2-lean solution is returned to the upper section of the absorber and is reused for CO2 absorption. Pump-arounds have been employed to remove heat generated by the exothermic absorption reactions among NH3, CO2, and water in the absorber and cool down to condense ammonia in the upper part of the regenerator. They are withdrawn at a low part of each packing section and pumped back into the same column at an elevated part. The temperature control in the column is the essential to maintain the CO2 removal efficiency.

texts.orig: Washing water is supplied to the top of each tower (absorber and regenerator) to keep liquid droplet and ammonia vapor from being released to the atmosphere. Used washing water is pumped to the concentrator to separate ammonia and water for the chemical recycling. The concentrated ammonia (vaporized ammonia) leaving the concentrator is supplied to a lower section of the regenerator. The bottom temperature of the concentrator is slightly higher than 100 ◦ C. Thus produced water, purified water after distillation, is reused for the ammonia washing.

The supply pressure of P steam into the pilot facility is about 10 kgf /cm 2 g; g represents gauge pressure.

texts.orig: Units related to CO2 purification and liquefaction were integrated to the absorption system to fully furnish the capture process. Captured CO2, the product stream, undergoes the purification unit to remove the impurities such as hydrogen cyanide (HCN), hydrogen sulfide (H2S) and water. Then small portion of the purified CO2 gas, ∼ 30%, is liquefied and stored in a tank.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Results of some of the short test runs are reported and discussed. Each test typically runs about 100 h without interruption. Control variables during typical runs were the flow rate of circulating absorbent, temperatures of lean solution into the absorber and rich solution to the stripper. Other process variables are the steam type, temperature of the rich solution to the stripper, pressure within the absorber, the coolant temperature, and so on.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The specifications and operation conditions of the CO2 capture facility were summarized in Table 2. The details for the developed process can be found in Rhee et al. (2011). The flow rate of feeding gas was maintained at 1000-1050 Nm 3 /h. The temperature and pressure of BFG pumped into the absorber by a blower were 35-50 ◦ C and 500-700 mmAq (gauge pressure), respectively. For the pressurized absorption the absorber was pressurized to 4000 mmAq (or 0.4 bar g). The typical circulation flow rate of aqueous ammonia of 7-9 wt% was 10,500 kg/h, which depends on the operating condition, but varied for different feed load and liquid to gas ratio (L/G ratio). L/G ratio was defined as the ratio of the circulating liquid flow rate in kg/h divided by the feed gas flow rate in Nm 3 /h. Temperatures and pressures are measured on-line along the process flow. Flow rates for water, absorbent solution and steam consumption were measured with flow meters (Yokogawa).

texts.orig: Key performance parameters were obtained during the test runs; CO2 removal efficiency, purity of product CO2 and thermal energy requirements. The technical and economic performance parameters were obtained by maintaining the CO2 recovery at certain level, typically over 90%. In some cases runs were conducted to measure the technical performance without maintaining the CO2 removal efficiency. CO2 production rate was obtained by CO2 removal efficiency multiplied by the CO2 flow rate in the inlet BFG stream. Normalized steam consumption was obtained by the steam consumption flow rate divided by the CO2 production rate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The profiles of CO2 concentration in the inlet and outlet BFG stream and the product stream are figured in Fig. 4(a) utilizing P steam or G steam. The efficiency of the CO2 removal could reach over 95% and the concentration of CO2 in the product stream was over 99%. The calorific value of BFG would be increased by 30% due to the selective removal of CO2 in the inlet BFG. The increase in calorific value will increase the efficiency of the power plant. Also, the process volume of BFG for combustion in the power plant could be significantly decreased, which, in turn, will lead to a cost benefit. Fig. 4(b) shows the CO concentrations in the inlet and outlet BFG stream, which clearly reveal the increased heating value of BFG after CO2 removal.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Steam for regenerating the absorbent solutions can be provided by either P steam or G steam or both. Steam supply configuration was constructed that when the quality of G steam required for regeneration is lower than the design value, for example 1 kgf /cm 2 g and 120 ◦ C, process steam will be supplied to the system. G steam was generated by recovering the waste heat of flue gas leaving the boiler stack at around 150 ◦ C. We tested various steam usage combinations including the two extreme cases; full usage of P steam or G steam. Measured concentration data were used to calculate CO2 removal efficiency and we found that even in the case of 100% usage of G steam as regeneration energy the CO2 removal efficiency were quite good. Therefore, we concluded that the steam required for the regeneration of the absorbent solution and the concentration of ammonia, can be supplied by the waste heat recovery system.

texts.orig: At the atmospheric absorption/regeneration condition, the ammonia concentration in the gas phase leaving the regenerator or in the CO2 production stream was lower than 10 ppm. However, the gaseous ammonia concentration in the absorber off-gas ( ∼ 200 ppm) had been higher than that of the regenerator. We observed that if the absorption is slightly pressurized up to 0.4 bar g, the gaseous concentration of ammonia leaving the absorber was also found to be less than 10 ppm (figure not shown). The ammonia concentrations leaving the facility can be controlled by adjusting the washing water flow rate or other process improvement such as the employment of pump-around (Takahashi, 2012). Conclusively, we found that applying a moderate pressurization of the absorption column can drastically reduce the gaseous ammonia loss.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Accepting that the process economics should be evaluated based on long-term operation data, we provide a rough estimate of an operating cost based on the current test results. While the operating cost of the CO2 capture facility is composed of steam requirement, electricity consumption for pumps and cooling tower operation, process water consumption, and chemical loss, etc., the majority of operating cost comes from the steam requirement for regeneration and concentration.

The observed phenomenon is due to the fact that the circulating absorbent flow and washing (or washed) water flow cannot be strictly disconnected. Therefore, one can insist that reducing the regeneration energy does not necessarily reduce the total thermal energy requirement.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Process improvements
texts.text: 4.3. Process improvements

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We address several issues for the further development of the ammonia-based CO2 capture in this section. Since we have confirmed the technical feasibility of the ammonia-based CO2 capture process, the next step forward is the reduction of thermal energy requirement which accordingly leads to the improvement of process economics. Suggestions for the improvement of technical performance together with the process economics can be achieved through the reduction of thermal energy requirement employing advanced thermal integration. The key component associated with the thermal energy requirement is the ammonia slip, since it has to be regulated to certain level while minimizing the thermal energy consumption. Conclusively, it can be said that reducing the thermal energy consumption is directly related to the suppression of ammonia slip. Therefore, we insist that methods to separate the vapor ammonia effectively from the aqueous mixture should be developed with different energy field not just the thermal energy field.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most salient advantage of the aqueous ammonia process over the conventional amine-based CO2 capture processes is that it can operate at lower temperatures (80-85 ◦ C) than that of the amine process ( ∼ 120 ◦ C) in the regenerator. Therefore, the critical characteristics of the aqueous ammonia process will be realized when lowand mid-temperature waste heat is available. Noting that the ironand steel-making industry has a lot of unused waste heat at lowand mid-temperatures ( ∼ 150 to ∼ 300 ◦ C), we insist that the ammonia-based CO2 capture process is an economically viable solution to the significant reduction of CO2 emissions in iron and steel industry. Our study has revealed that he aqueous ammonia process can be used for the CO2 capture where the CO2 concentration is high enough, such as over 20% and waste heat is available. In that sense, another ideal target workplace for the purpose can be cement industry, where typical CO2 concentration in off-gas streams is 25% with abundant unrecovered waste heat such as steams leaving the preheater of the raw materials, clinker cooler and rotary kiln.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ammonia-based CO2 capture pilot facility has been constructed and successfully operated at POSCO-Pohang Works, Republic of Korea. We summarized the operation results from a pilot plant with the capacity of 1000 Nm 3 -BFG/h (or 10 t-CO2/d) including CO2 removal efficiency and the purity of product CO2 gas stream. Steam generated from waste heat recovery system (G steam) has been successfully supplied to the facility for the regeneration of absorbent solution and the ammonia concentration. From the test operations we found that the CO2 removal efficiency could exceed 90% and the CO2 purity was over 99% (liquid CO2 > 99.9%). We confirmed the stable operation using G steam as the regeneration energy. Ammonia concentration in gaseous phase can be controlled under 10 ppm at the both tops of absorber and regenerator. The thermal energy consumption in the regenerator was found to be less than 2.5 GJ/t-CO2, while that in the concentrator was less than 2.0 GJ/tCO2. Based on previous and current experimental results, we insist that the process concept is technically proven and economically feasible with the waste heat recovery system. The key feature of the process can be extremely manifested when waste heat at low temperatures is available. Two exemplary industrial areas are the iron and cement industries where waste heat at low temperature is available.
